coredata.dc:title: Wood-cellulose photoluminescence material based on carbon quantum dot for light conversion
coredata.dc:creator: Gan, Jian
Wu, Yan
Yang, Feng
Zhang, Haiqiao
Wu, Xinyu
Wang, Yajing
Xu, Rui
coredata.dc:description: To expand the application of transparent wood (TW) in the field of photoluminescence, lignin-based carbon quantum dots (CDs) were impregnated into wood cellulose template (CT) as a fluorescent functional material, the photoluminescent transparent wood (PTW) with light conversion was prepared. The results showed that FTW with different wavelength emissions could be obtained by controlling the CDs' conjugated size and surface functional groups. The loading of CDs does not affect the excellent transmittance (86.1%) and optical haze (73%) of TW. Compared to the original wood, the tensile properties of PTW have been enhanced from 368 MPa to 422 MPa. By controlling the content of different CDs, a PTW for white light conversion was prepared with the CIE color coordinates of (0.29, 0.34). This study is a new attempt to apply CDs to TW, providing a broad prospect for uniform light conversion and color display.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Cellulose
Transparent wood
Optical properties

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis and characterizations of CDs doped with N elements
Structural and morphological characterizations of CDs
Chemical composition and bonds characterization of CDs
Application of PTW to light conversion layers

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Synthetic schematic diagram of three types of CDs.
TEM images of B-CDs (A), G-CDs (B), O-CDs (C); High-resolution TEM images of B-CDs (D), G-CDs (E), O-CDs (F), and corresponding lateral size distributions of B-CDs, G-CDs, O-CDs (G).
XRD patterns (A) of B-CDs, G-CDs, and O-CDs; Raman spectra of three types of CDs (B) and Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra of three CDs.
XPS spectra of B-CDs, G-CDs, and O-CDs (A); High-resolution XPS fitting results for C 1s, (B, C, D), and O 1s spectra (E, F, G) of B-CDs, G-CDs, and O-CDs, respectively.

UV–vis absorption spectra (A) of B-CDs, G-CDs, and O-CDs; Normalized PL spectra of B-CDs (Ex = 336), G-CDs (Ex = 395) and O-CDs (Ex = 430) (B); Photographs of B-CDs, G-CDs, and O-CDs under 365 nm UV light (C); Fluorescence intensity attenuation of three types of CDs upon irradiation with UV light at 365 nm (D) and excitation-dependent fluorescence emission of B-CDs, G-CDs and O-CDs (E).

SEM image of the cell wall of origin wood (OW) (A) and wood cellulose template (CT) (B, C) in different magnification; the content of three major elements of OW and CT (D); SEM image of CT impregnated with CDs (E) and the EDS of N-element of CDs nanoparticles in CT (F).

SEM image of PTW in different magnification (A); Optical transmittance of PTW (B) and optical haze of PTW (C) (insert: photos of PTW in daylight); Schematic to illustrate mechanical tests of R and L directions for wood (D); Stress-strain curves for the OW, UV resin and PTW in L-direction (E) and R-original (F), respectively; transparency and haze display of PTW in sunlight (G).

The schematic diagram of the PTW used for the fluorescence conversion (A); CIE coordinates of B-PTW, G-PTW, and O-PTW and photographs of B-PTW, G-PTW, and O-PTW under 365 nm UV light (B); PL spectra of W-CDs (C); CIE coordinates of W-PTW and Photographs of W-PTW (D).

head.ce:title: Wood-cellulose photoluminescence material based on carbon quantum dot for light conversion

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Furnishings and Industrial Design, Nanjing Forestry University, Nanjing 210037, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Co-Innovation Center of Efficient Processing and Utilization of Forest Resources

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Co-Innovation Center of Efficient Processing and Utilization of Forest Resources, Nanjing Forestry University, Nanjing 210037, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: College of Furnishings and Industrial Design, Nanjing Forestry University, Nanjing 210037, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Furnishings and Industrial Design

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: To expand the application of transparent wood (TW) in the field of photoluminescence, lignin-based carbon quantum dots (CDs) were impregnated into wood cellulose template (CT) as a fluorescent functional material, the photoluminescent transparent wood (PTW) with light conversion was prepared. The results showed that FTW with different wavelength emissions could be obtained by controlling the CDs' conjugated size and surface functional groups. The loading of CDs does not affect the excellent transmittance (86.1%) and optical haze (73%) of TW. Compared to the original wood, the tensile properties of PTW have been enhanced from 368 MPa to 422 MPa. By controlling the content of different CDs, a PTW for white light conversion was prepared with the CIE color coordinates of (0.29, 0.34). This study is a new attempt to apply CDs to TW, providing a broad prospect for uniform light conversion and color display.

ce:section.ce:para: Glass and plastic-based transparent film materials or substrates are most widely used in optoelectronics (Wen et al., 2017). The functional light conversion transparent film containing ultraviolet (UV)-excited active fluorescent substances is an essential component in many optoelectronic devices. An extensive example of this would be the white light-emitting diodes (Woo Jang et al., 2018). The blue emitting InGaN-based chips can realize the conversion of red and green emission by encapsulating in a transparent cover layer that contains active nano-fluorescent materials. Most commercial fluorescent active materials are phosphors doped with rare-earth-based nanoparticles and semiconductor quantum dots (QDs) (Shang et al., 2014). Usually, such active nano-fluorescent materials are dispersed in high-transmittance polymers or silica to achieve a high concentration of central fluorescence close to the excitation chip. In addition, these packaged light-emitting diodes (LEDs) are combined with passive diffuse reflectors to achieve uniform illumination (Pust et al., 2014). The extensive use of petroleum-based polymers and silicon is not aligned with the vision of “carbon neutrality” and sustainable development.

Transparent wood (TW) is prepared by a cellulose skeleton of delignified wood impregnated with an optically transparent polymer matrix with a similar refractive index (Li et al., 2017). Such as Hu et al. removed lignin from natural woods by sodium hydroxide, sodium sulfite, and hydrogen peroxide, and then fabricated a series of transparent wood by impregnated with epoxy resin (Mi et al., 2020; Tanpichai et al., 2022; Xia et al., 2021). The bio-based composite material assembled by cellulose and polymeric substance has robust mechanical properties, optical transmittance, and low thermal conductivity, which makes it conforms to the ideas of sustainable, transparent materials, and can be applied in the field of optoelectronics (Jiang et al., 2021; Tang et al., 2022; Y. Wu, J. Zhou, et al., 2020).

Nanoscale carbon quantum dots (CDs), as a new kind of luminescent material, have attracted extensive attention because of their unique optical properties (tunable emission and photostability) and biocompatibility (non-toxicity) (Xu et al., 2020). In the field of LEDs, this new class of nano-luminescent material is considered to be a potential substitute for rare-earth-based luminescent nanoparticles (Jiang et al., 2015; Li et al., 2020). However, the synthesis of CDs in the red emission wavelength segment is still an inherent challenge (Liu, Gou, et al., 2020; Liu, Yang, et al., 2020; Wang, Wang, et al., 2020). Given that an adjustable process can prepare CDs and that their emission wavelengths can be extended across the entire visible spectrum (especially red emission), CDs can be extended to be an excellent building block for optoelectronic devices, especially white-LEDs (Feng et al., 2015; Wang, Li, et al., 2020; Wang et al., 2017). To this end, some attempts have been reported to obtain the red emission CDs by using different precursors and complicated column chromatography postprocessing techniques in the past few years (Wang, Yu, et al., 2020; Wang et al., 2017; Wei et al., 2021). Although the improved full-color emissions of these CDs promote them an advantage in the performance of optoelectronic devices, most reported application of the CDs for its effective full-color emission was just simply mixed with a polymer matrix or silica gel to prevent aggregation-caused quenching (ACQ) of CDs (Peng et al., 2018; Wei et al., 2020). The use of petroleum-based polymers and silicon does not echo the perspective of green, sustainable development and simple synthesis techniques to prepare robust CDs with adjustable photoluminescence (PL) emission will inevitably require a practical method.

Furthermore, lignin is one of the most widespread biomass materials in nature, and it is generally collected from waste black liquor downstream in the paper industry (Jiang et al., 2021; Li et al., 2022). Lignin is one of the most suitable materials for preparing CDs (Wang, Wang, et al., 2020). The complex aromatic structure and various sorts of active Oxygen-containing functional groups can provide favorable conditions for forming conjugated graphite cores and the surface functionalization of CDs. In addition, the natural hierarchical porous structure of cellulose skeleton in delignified wood can be used as a solid-state matrix to embed organic dye molecules or quantum dots in it, which can effectively eliminate the aggregation-caused quenching ACD of CDs (Li et al., 2022; Yang et al., 2021). The rigid cellulose framework in TW provides dense hydrogen bonding sites and suitable oxygen barriers to reduce the nonradiative transition, thus producing a uniform fluorescence emission (Chao et al., 2021; Liu, Gou, et al., 2020; Liu, Yang, et al., 2020). For delignified wood, this is the return of lignin in another new form. The excellent optical haze and diffusely transmitting are the most promising for its development and applications in optoelectronics (Bi et al., 2018).

Our previous works developed transparent wood by delignifying poplar (Populus L.) wood (Wu, Wang, & Yang, 2021; Y. Wu, J. Zhou, et al., 2020). Here, we reported a CDs-Cellulose composite material based on poplar wood. The CDs with tunable and stable fluorescence emission from blue to orange were prepared using lignin as the precursor and poplar-based transparent wood as a substrate to develop a photoluminescent transparent wood (PTW). By controlling the doping of different nitrogen-containing compounds, CDs exhibits wavelength emission of different colors. Based on this, we fabricate all kinds of colorful PTW, including white light. High transparency (>86%) and haze (>70%) indicate its excellent light-scattering ability, which makes it practical in uniform lighting and colorful display.

The poplar (Populus L.) wood chips with dimensions 20 mm × 20 mm × 1.5 mm (length × width × thickness) were provided by Yihua Lifestyle Technology Co., Ltd. (Guangdong, China). Kraft lignin with low sulfonate content was purchased by Sigma-Aldrich (Shanghai, China). Urea was purchased from Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Nanjing, China). The p-phenylenediamine (p-PDA), terephthalic acid (TPA), m-phenylenediamine (m-PDA), sodium chlorite (NaClO2, 80%), and glacial acetic acid (CH3COOH) were supplied by Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Other reagents such as sulfur acid (H2SO4, AR, 98%), anhydrous ethanol (AR) were purchased from Nanjing Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Nanjing, China).

According to the previous experiment, the delignified poplar wood matrix was prepared using the chlorine oxidation method to remove lignin from wood (Y. Wu, J. C. Zhou, et al., 2020). The poplar wood chips were immersed in a mixed solution of 4% NaClO2 and CH3COOH and heated in a water bath at 85 °C for 4 h. Control the remaining amount of NaClO2 in the reaction system to keep the oxidizing chlorine with an appropriate concentration until the color of poplar chips was bleached to pure white. The treated samples were washed with ultrapure water to remove the chemical residue. The prepared delignified poplar chips were stored in a mixture of absolute ethanol and acetone to remove the residual moisture therein, to strengthen the rigidity of the cellulose skeleton.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterizations of CDs doped with N elements
ce:section.ce:para: Colorful single emission CDs were prepared by hydrothermal carbonization of lignin with different Nitrogen-containing compounds at the same reaction temperature. Typically, 100 mg of kraft lignin and 300 mg of urea were dissolved in 10 mL of deionized water (DI). The mixture was transferred into a 50 mL poly (p-phenylene)-lined stainless-steel autoclave and heated at 160 °C for 8 h. After the reaction solution was cool to room temperature, centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 10 min to remove the solid using a high-speed centrifuge. The supernatant after centrifugation was dialyzed in DI (1000 Da molecular weight cut off) for 48 h to remove small molecules. Then, the purified solution was freeze-dried to yield blue carbon quantum dots (B-CD) powder. Other CDs were prepared using a similar preparation procedure to B-CD, except for the different solvent, heating temperature, and time. Generally, the 100 mg kraft lignin was used to react with 300 mg p-PDA in 10 mL of H2SO4 (40 wt%) and heated at 200 °C for 8 h for green carbon quantum dots (G-CD), and 100 mg kraft lignin was used to react with 300 mg m-PDA and 300 mg TPA in 10 mL of H2SO4 and heated at 200 °C for 10 h for orange carbon quantum dots (O-CDs.)

The vacuum impregnation method was used to uniformly disperse the CDs in poplar wood's hierarchical cellulose network structure. Specifically, the prepared wood cellulose template (CT) was placed in different CDs ethanol dispersion with a 0.01 g/mL concentration in a vacuum for 30 min. Then, the poplar wood chip impregnated with CDs was placed in the UV curing resin and immersed again for 30 min in a vacuum until the poplar wood changed transparently. Finally, the poplar wood chip was taken out and placed in a UV light (3Kw) for 3 min to obtain the PTW. For the preparation of warm-light PTW, the process was different, specifically 0.1 g B-CDs, 0.1 g G-CDs, and 0.1 g O-CDs were added in 10 mL ethanol to obtain the white-light CDs dispersion.

The microscopic size and lattice spacing of CDs under different magnifications were observed by JEM 2100 (JEOL, Japan) transmission electron microscope (TEM). X-ray diffraction was used to investigate the crystalline phase of CDs by using Ultima IV multifunctional horizontal X-ray diffractometer (XRD) (Rigaku, Japan). Raman spectrum was recorded on a DXR 532 Raman spectrometer using a 532 nm laser beam (Themor, USA). The surface functional groups of dried CDs were recognized with a FTIR infrared spectrometer (VERTEX 80 V, Karlsruhe, Germany). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra of CD samples were also acquired using an Ultima IV spectrometer (AXIS UltraDLD, Shimadzu, UK). UV-spectrophotometer (U-3900, Hitachi, Japan) was used to test the UV–visible absorption spectra absorption spectrum of the CDs in an aqueous solution. The CDs' photoluminescent spectra and absolute quantum yield (QY) were recorded using an FLS1000 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Edinburgh Instruments, UK) in aqueous dispersion. The microscopic morphology of the PTW was observed using the Quanta 200 scanning electron microscope (SEM, FEI, USA), combining energy dispersive spectrometer (EDS) to characterize the types of elements on the surface of the sample. A Shimadzu AGS-X tester was used to conduct the stress-strain characterization for the PTW and control sample. The photographs were taken with a camera (FUJIFILM X-T3) under UV light excited at 365 nm (5 W).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural and morphological characterizations of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 1 showed the synthesis process of CDs, and different Nitrogen-containing compounds were used to hybridize with lignin to control the degree of graphitization. To understand the factors affecting the tunable optical properties of CDs, the microscopic morphology and crystal structure were carried out.

As shown in Fig. 2 , three types of CDs demonstrate quasi-spherical with a rather well dispersibility. The B-CDs, G-CDs, and O-CDs showed average sizes of 1.8 nm, 2.3 nm, and 3.0 nm, and the standard deviation of sizes was 0.2 nm, 0.3 nm, and 0.4 nm, respectively. From the size distribution of CDs in Fig. 2G, it could be confirmed that three types of CDs samples were indeed of quantum level. Different acid solvents were used to promote the amount of dehydration between the lignin and Nitrogen-containing compounds while grafting different benzene ring structures to the aromatic skeleton to expand the size of sp2 domains. With increasing the size of CDs, the emission color of CDs exhibited a red-shift characteristic. High-resolution TEM images in Fig. 2D, E, and F demonstrated the high degree of crystallinity of the three types of CDs. Three types of CDs had round shapes. In the meantime, well-resolved lattice fringes of these CDs were clearly observed. And the lattice fringe distance was 0.21 nm for B-CDs and G-CDs, which corresponded to the crystal phase of graphene (100). Especially for O-CDs, a typical honeycomb-like crystalline pattern was observed at a high magnification level (Fig. s1 supporting information). The lattice fringe distance was 0.34 nm, corresponding to the (002) plane of graphite, indicating that the dots typically consist of a few layers of nano-graphite.

XRD images in Fig. 3A demonstrated the crystallinity of CDs. A broad XRD peak was observed at the central position (25°), which indicated the partial graphitization and small size of the CDs. The peak at 26.4° is sharp for O-CDs, which is the (002) diffraction peak of the graphitic structure. The graphite structures of these CDs are reflected in the Raman spectrum (Fig. 3B), the intensity ratios of the G band at 1600 cm−1 and the D band at 1380 cm−1 (IG/ID) of the B-CDs, G-CDs, and O-CDs were 1.04, 1.17 and 1.38, respectively. An increase in the ratio values means a strengthen in the degree of graphitization of the CDs core, consistent with the characteristics of an increase in particle size.

These analyses provide essential theoretical progress for constructing multiple emission CDs using lignin as a precursor. The introduction of the Nitrogen-containing compound with the aromatic structure increases conjugated structure, which effectively controls the degree of graphitization of the CDs core, thereby achieving a visible increase in the particle size and an adjustable red-shift emission.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Chemical composition and bonds characterization of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The degree of dehydration and carbonization of the CDs core and surface oxidation was modulated by adjusting the amount of Nitrogen doping, the reaction time, and temperature. The FT-IR and XPS spectra were carried out to characterize the CDs' chemical element composition and functional groups. As shown in Fig. 3C, the B-CDs, G-CDs, and O-CDs had similar infrared spectra, which indicated that similar chemical compositions exist in three types of CDs.

In the spectra of these CDs, it could be seen that the broad absorption peaks belonged to OH/NH around 3450 cm−1, and other characteristic functional groups, such as CN at 1627 cm−1, sp2 CC at 1465 cm−1, and the stretching vibrations of CO at 1123 cm−1, CN at 1205 cm−1, showed a certain strong absorption peak.

The XPS analysis was used to support the infrared spectrum date further. In Fig. 4 , the three typical peaks at 284.5 eV, 302.4 eV, and 533.3 eV belonged to C 1s, N 1s, and O 1s, respectively, demonstrating the B-CDs, G-CDs, and O-CDs contain the same elemental composition.

As shown in Fig. 4B, C, and D, the XPS C 1s spectra of three samples can be deconvoluted into three peaks, relevant to CC\CC (284.7 eV), CN (285.4 eV), and CO (286.8 eV), and the O 1s spectra can be deconvoluted into two peaks at 532.5 eV and 533.7 eV, which correspond to OH and CO. The XPS spectra of the CDs further confirm the conclusion of the FTIR. Besides, the increasing area ratio of the sp2 CC/CC peaks also illustrated the gradually increasing dehydration and carbonization degree for the B-CDs, G-CDs, and O-CDs. As shown in O 1s spectra, the electron-donating OH group content significantly declines from B-CDs to O-CDs. In contrast, the amount of electron-withdrawing COOH groups notable increased from 43.1% (B-CDs) to 72.6% (O-CDs), which indicated the surface oxidation of the CDs could boost the red-shift of PL wavelengths. As with particle size, the chemical composition and proportion are also believed to be responsible for affecting the PL performance of the CDs (Ding et al., 2018; Wang, Yu, et al., 2020), but the specific mechanism required to be discussed in more depth.

By controlling the precursor and reaction conditions, we have gained multi-emission CDs. The absolute quantum yield was used to evaluate the PL properties of the prepared CDs. The tested optical QYs for the B-CDs, G-CDs, and O-CDs are 41.41%, 14.42%, and 12.09%, respectively (Fig. s2 supporting information). As shown in Fig. 5A, the UV–vis absorption spectra of three CDs exhibited two prominent optical absorption peaks at 216 nm and 270 nm, corresponding to the electron transition π → π ∗ of the CC and n → π ∗ of the CN, respectively. It could be seen from Fig. 5B that while the excitation wavelengths of the B-CDs, G-CDs, and O-CDs gradually shifted to longer wavelengths, the PL emission wavelengths also gradually shifted red, specifically 395 nm, 515 nm, and 602 nm, corresponding to blue, green and orange, respectively. The inherent mechanism for the wavelength red-shift is generally attributed to the increase in sp2 conjugation (Qu et al., 2016; Wang, Yu, et al., 2020). Fig. 5C shows fluorescence images of these CDs phosphors under UV light (365 nm), and the emissions are red-shifted from blue to orange for the samples of B-CDs to O-CDs.

In Fig. 5E, for the CDs, when the excitation wavelength changes from 330 to 430 nm, the emission peaks' position was not shifted and remained at the respective emission positions. The PL wavelengths of the CDs exhibited independence from excitation, which was attributed to the relatively uniform size of CDs and the same emission sites on its surface, which is jointly affected by the conjugation effect of its surface functional groups and carbon cores. With the increase of excitation wavelength, the emission intensity of three CDs all showed the characteristics of first increasing and then decreasing. And we have obtained the maximum excitation wavelengths for the CDs, which were 340 nm, 395 nm, and 440 nm, respectively. Furthermore, these as-prepared CDs showed excellent PL stability. When they were irradiated by UV at 395 nm, just a slight decrease in fluorescence intensity (6% to 9%, Fig. 5D) occurred after they were photobleached for 12 h. We further evaluated the pH stability of these as-prepared CDs. The results (Fig. s3 supporting information) showed that the emission intensity of these CDs revealed the neglected change in the pH value range of 1–12, suggesting the incredible pH stability of these CDs. The good pH stability may be attributed to the conjugate effect between the functional groups and carbon core, inhibiting the protonation-deprotonation process.

Wood is mainly composed of cellulose, hemicellulose and lignin. Delignification makes the porous structure composed of wood cell walls more clearly exposed (Cai et al., 2021). Studies believe that the primary strategy to obtain transparent wood was to remove lignin selectively, the coloring substance of wood, then fill the porous lumen space of fibrous tube and the vessel cells with substances of similar refractive index (Liu et al., 2018). Besides, the removal of lignin leaves more hydrogen bonds in the three-dimensional structure, which is conducive to the dispersion of CDs in the cellulose skeleton (Wu et al., 2019). From the comparison chart of the content of three major elements of wood in Fig. 6D, it could be seen that the relative content of lignin in the delignified wood template was significantly reduced. In contrast, the relative content of cellulose has increased, which indicated that lignin was effectively removed. As can be seen from the FTIR spectra (Fig. s4 supporting information) of origin wood and cellulose template, the absorption peaks of the original wood include the stretching vibration of the -OH group at 3331 cm−1, the stretching vibration of the CH group at 2930 cm−1, the stretching vibration of CO group at 1740 cm−1, the stretching vibration of aromatic ring skeleton group of lignin at 1509 cm−1 and the stretching vibration of CO group at 1034 cm−1. The cellulose template retains the characteristic peaks of wood cellulose, and the characteristic peak belonging to the aromatic skeleton group of lignin at 1509 cm−1 disappears, which indicates that the lignin in the cellulose template is basically removed. In addition, the relative intensities of the OH peaks in the cellulose template were found to be improved, which also indicated an increasing of favorable hydroxyl groups in it.

From the microscopic image of the sample in Fig. 6 A, B, and C, compared with the control sample, the tube holes and cell cavities in the CT were clearer. At high magnification, it could be observed that gaps appeared between the cell walls, and the thickness of the cell walls was also reduced, which enhanced the internal space of the wood. As shown in Fig. 6E, when CDs was dispersed in the wood, the microstructure is no obviously different from CT. To observe the distribution of CDs in CT, the characterization of EDS was applied to the surface of the CT impregnated with CDs. In Fig. 6F, the dense yellow dots were derived by the N element of CDs nanoparticles, which displays CDs are evenly distributed in the CT.

A binary mixed resin of urethane acrylate and epoxy acrylate, which can be cured under UV light, was used as a filler to prepare PTW. Compared with the traditional epoxy resin and polymethyl methacrylate, UV curing resin has a simple curing process and short curing time (Wu, Gan, & Wu, 2021; Wu, Gan, et al., 2020; Wu, Wu, et al., 2020; Yan & Wang, 2020). Furthermore, the cross-linking reaction in the UV curing process increases the resin saturation and reduces the oxidative yellowing defect common in other TWs (Chandra & Crosby, 2011; Decker et al., 2004). It can be seen that the filling of resin changed the appearance of CT from white to transparent. At the micro-level in Fig. 7A, all opened channels of CDs-Cellulose were occupied by UV resin. The cell walls and tube holes inside the wood were tightly glued together by resin, which was the source of transparency and can significantly improve the mechanical properties of PTW. Fig. 8B shows the optical transmittance of PTW. Compared with the original wood, the light transmittance of PTW had been greatly improved, with a light transmittance of 86.1%. The experimental stress-strain curves in Fig. 8D, E, F show the different anisotropic mechanical properties of PTW in R and L directions. It can be concluded that, compared with UV resin, the fracture strength and toughness of PTW has expressed a significant improvement (reach at 450 MPa) in the L direction. In addition, in the R direction, PTW has inherited the excellent ductility of the original wood and even improved, and the breaking strength has been significantly improved (65.4 MPa). From the microscopic analysis, this can be attributed to the binding effect of the impregnated resin on the wood fibers arranged in the horizontal direction. Surprisingly, in the L and R directions, PTW perfectly demonstrates the combination of the mechanical properties of the two substrates.

It also could be seen in Fig. 7A that the cell wall and tube hole could be well preserved. The subtle difference in refractive index and the shrinkage crack between the cell wall and resin can account for the high optical haze of TW. In Fig. 7C, with the red-shift of the wavelength, the penetrating power of light is enhanced, which brings about a reduction in PTW haze. In general, compared with UV resin (haze is about 1%), the high haze of PTW maintains the entire visible light wavelength range, up to 73%. The photos inserted in Fig. 7B, C, and Fig. 7G show that PTW has good transparency and haze in sunlight.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of PTW to light conversion layers
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the photoluminescence properties of three CDs, we apply them as light conversion layers for different colors. Fig. 8A displays the schematic diagram of the PTW used for the light conversion layer. The LED UV excitation chip was used as the central excitation light at the bottom, and different light conversion layers were covered on it, respectively. Typically, when the LED chip was activated, the emitted UV light was partially absorbed by the down-conversion layer, causing the down-conversion layer to re-emit blue light. The emitted blue light will excite the next light conversion layer to re-emit green or orange light. By adjusting the ratio of the CDs content of different conversion layers, the light of different colors can be finally obtained.

Fig. 8C shows the physical photo of three PTWs containing different CDs. These PTWs as composite light conversion layers are optically transparent under natural light and exhibit different emissions at the corresponding excitation wavelength. These emissions are the same as those of the CDs immersed in it, confirming that they were evenly dispersed in the TW, preventing ACQ. In addition, we further evaluated the CIE color coordinates of different PTWs under the corresponding excitation, and the CIE coordinates of the CDs are (0.166, 0.101), (0.272, 0.432), and (0.628, 0.370), respectively. These PTW films indicate a broad range of PL emission colors under UV light at 365 nm, contributing to the construction of light conversion layers of different colors, even white light.

By controlling the relative content of B-CDs, G-CDs and O-CDs dispersed in the PTW, respectively, the white PTW (W-PTW) that could convert white light had been obtained. Specifically, the mass ratio of the three CDs was 1:1.6:0.8. As shown in Fig. 8D, the spectrum of the white CDs showed a wider peak shape across the visible light area, and emission bands centered at 452 nm, 520 nm, and 602 nm could be seen, respectively. The W-PTW also showed the color coordinates of (0.293 0.341) and exhibited uniform white light under a 365 nm UV lamp (Fig. 8D). Wholly, the stability results of the colorful PTWs based on CDs are promising candidates for uniform light conversion and colorful display application.

In summary, by controlling the dehydration and carbonization of lignin with different Nitrogen-containing compounds, the solvothermal method was used to synthesize CDs with high QY, multicolor-emission, and stable fluorescence performance. Then, disperse them in cellulose template with a porous structure to prepare photoluminescence transparent wood with blue, green, and orange emission colors, respectively. The as-prepared PTWs exhibited both excellent optical transparency (86.1%) and haze (73%). Besides, the mechanical properties have been improved compared to the original wood. Furthermore, the PTWs display an excellent multi-color luminous performance. The CIE coordinates of three PTWs were (0.166, 0.101), (0.272, 0.432), and (0.628, 0.370), corresponding to standard blue, green, and orange emission areas, respectively. Eventually, by adding more types of these CDs in appropriate ratios to the PTW, we have succeeded in fabricating the white-PTWs with the CIE coordinates of (0.293, 0.341). Excellent light scattering ability and adjustable colorful luminescence performance make PTW have a bright future in the field of uniform light-emission and fluorescence conversion.

Jian Gan: Conceptualization, Writing-Original draft preparation, data curation; Yan Wu: Conceptualization, Writing - Reviewing and Editing, Funding acquisition; Feng Yang: Funding acquisition; Haiqiao Zhang: Methodology; Xinyu Wu: Writing - Reviewing and Editing; Yajing Wang and Rui Xu: Software, Validation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare no conflicts of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledgment the financial support from the Peoples' Republic of China.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.carbpol.2022.119429.
